name: Ken Peplowski
abstract: Kenneth Joseph Peplowski (May 23, 1959 – February 2, 2026) was an American jazz clarinetist and tenor saxophonist. He was known primarily for playing swing music.

interviews.title: Ken Peplowski interviewed by Monk Rowe, Aspen, Colorado, October 10, 1997 [interview 1 of 2]
Ken Peplowski interviewed by Monk Rowe, via Zoom, June 9, 2020 [interview 2 of 2]

quotes.text: Well they did, there was a guy — a few years ago they used a Benny Goodman record on a Chase Manhattan bank commercial. And these ad agencies always like to imitate each other. If something is popular they all do it. So then all the clarinet players were getting calls. And they always wanted to do a recreation of “Sing Sing Sing” or something like that. But one guy had me and a drummer come in, and he had taken the old Carnegie Hall record of “Sing Sing Sing” and edited it the way he wanted it. And he wanted us to recreate this edit. But he had no sense of time. So the edit was chopped up so the drums would play like a bar and a half and then the clarinet would come in. It didn’t make any sense at all. And this is what he wanted us to play. There was no music written, and this guy was probably getting thousands and thousands of dollars for being the composer of this jingle. And it sounded about as goofy as it sounds from me telling it.
quotes.caption: Anecdote about a Benny Goodman commercial recreation
quotes.doc_title: Ken Peplowski interviewed by Monk Rowe, Aspen, Colorado, October 10, 1997 [interview 1 of 2]

about_quotes.speaker: Howard Alden
about_quotes.evidence: We’ve known each other almost fifteen years... And we play together quite often both in a duo situation... I love playing with Ken.
about_quotes.doc_title: Howard Alden interviewed by Monk Rowe, Aspen, Colorado, October 11, 1997

about_quotes.speaker: John Fedchock
about_quotes.evidence: Ken Peplowski, a clarinet player, was leading the band... he invited me back... let’s split off — you take a couple of nights and I’ll take a couple of nights.
about_quotes.doc_title: John Fedchock interviewed by Monk Rowe, Clinton, New York, May 28, 2015

about_quotes.speaker: Loren Schoenberg
about_quotes.evidence: my sax section was already set — it was set for a while. Chuck Wilson and Jack Stooky on altos, Ken Peplowski and Doug Lawrence on tenors, and Danny Bank.
about_quotes.doc_title: Loren Schoenberg interviewed by Monk Rowe, New York City, New York, January 14, 2019

about_quotes.speaker: Mat Domber
about_quotes.evidence: we have a record that’s going to be coming out very shortly, that we recorded a while ago in 2005 actually, of Kenny and Ken Peplowski.
about_quotes.doc_title: Mat Domber interviewed by Monk Rowe, New York City, New York, January 12, 2007

about_quotes.speaker: George Avakian
about_quotes.evidence: [INTERVIEWER] ...seems like the mainstream tradition that’s being carried on in jazz today is mostly the white musicians — Ken Peplowski and Randy — [INTERVIEWEE] Oh yes it’s certainly true.
about_quotes.doc_title: George Avakian interviewed by Monk Rowe, Clinton, New York, April 21, 1998

about_quotes.speaker: Michael Moore
about_quotes.evidence: Ken Peplowski is another one. I mean he probably has a better record collection... But he knows how he wants to play and what he loves is those songs, and carrying on that tradition.
about_quotes.doc_title: Michael Moore interviewed by Monk Rowe, Chautauqua, New York, September 12, 1997
